title: My personal resolution of the TV and movie endings
display_title: My personal resolution of the TV and movie endings
wikitext:
[]: My personal resolution of the TV and movie endings             Quote Post by V » Wed Oct 01, 2008 1:45 pm just felt like putting this into words, but my personal "resolution" of the alternate TV and movie endings runs thusly: first off, there is only one "Evangelion movie"; Death & Rebirth is an artifact of the series release history. Further, I have friends who literally haven't seen ANY of the movies, saying "well I don't want to sit through 2 or 3 whole movies that would take too long"....whereas if I had convinced them "there is only one movie to watch, for 2 hours" they'd have watched it. Death & Rebirth's continued release only confused a lot of people. as for the endings: After I watched episodes 1 through 24, off of Adult Swim, I then went to a friend's house and we watched End of Evangelion. I didn't even bother to watch the TV ending for months, having been warned that "it's just a clip show because they ran out of money" Upon further analysis, I've arrived at this position: Anyone who tells you that the TV ending was the "intended" ending, is selling something, or misinformed. So much evidence points to that, more or less what would become "End of Eva", was already what they had decided on (plus the shots of Misato and Ritsuko's deaths match End of Eva, the early concept art showing the Eva-02 vs MP Evas fight, etc.) Does this stop the TV ending, from nonetheless being an unplanned but still "Good" ending? .....my position is: They're interesting episodes but could never serve as an actual "ending" nor do I think they were even intended to: they announced they'd start work on End of Eva *two weeks* after the final TV episode aired. Thus, I don't really think they meant the TV ending to be the "final version" as for various Anno interviews defending it....most of those were made 15 years ago, right after the wave of vandalism/backlash against the show for the TV ending, and I think he was just feeling hurt. Other interviews with other Gainax folks seem to indicate that the whole "too bad" response was just to how people utterly rejected the TV ending. The big problem, more than anything else, is convincing people that this isn't like "Naruto" or whatever, where the movie ending is an "alternate ending" and the TV series ending stands on its own. It doesn't. The movie ending is the "real ending". Many major running plotlines were unresolved by the end of the TV series (i.e. frequent mentioning that they're building Mass Production Evas....) The show doesn't "End when the final Angel dies"; throughout the series its hinted that the REAL bad guys are Nerv/Seele. Essentially, my stated view is that Gainax wanted to make End of Eva, but couldn't, so what the final 2 TV episodes are is basically they cut out a large section of the "Instrumentality/Third Impact sequence" from the last quarter of End of Eva, and made it a TV episode. You could literally edit the final 2 TV episodes into End of Eva during the Third Impact sequence, and no one would call it "incongruent" it's sort of like an advanced preview of the more esoteric parts of End of Eva and was only meant to tide fans over until the movie came out There are many answers out there I've seen for "how to reconcile they endingss"; some saying that they are alternate endings, some that they are the same "ending" viewed differently. I've noticed Aaron's position that, if you look at them, they cannot possibly be the same ending; I enjoy the analogy of Shinji's acceptance of Instrumentality in the TV ending, to Winston Smith's acceptance of Big Brother at the end of 1984. But you see, I think that was looking at it wrong, and I have a more nuanced view than what Aaron said: these aren't "alternate endings" but they're not "the same ending" either My position, is that End of Evangelion is the only Ending The "last 2 TV episodes" were never meant to be an "Ending" The 2 TV episodes are as if you took a huge chunk of the Instrumentality scene from EoE and aired it as a TV episode, out of context... ...with the key destinction that they do NOT include the last 10 minutes of the intended, EoE ending. Basically they were sort of meant to keep the audience hanging....AGAIN because EoE was announced barely 2 weeks after the TV series ended..... and the cliffhanger was "wait, he ACCEPTED Instrumentality?" the TV series ending has been called "happier" by some because we "evolve to a higher plane" but that's not really what happened and really, to me, its the most horrifying thing: compare Cypher and Neo's views on "The Matrix"; Cypher wants to be back in the make-believe happy-world where all is well; Neo prefers the world where there's a lot of fear and pain, but it is *Real* So the Instrumentality-world is basically the fake happiness of "I could never learn to overcome my personality flaws and connect with other people, despite the pain, so the only answer was to jerry-rig God and basically unite everyone into One Big Happy Hive Mind of Nirvana-nothingness", everything "harmonizing" to create....nothing but that's a fake happiness but more importantly; the whole series was about Shinji's need to learn to connect with other people....so the TV ending's Instrumentality was basically throwing all of that away and saying "guess what? that was impossible, lets give up on that" its just incongruent with Shinji's character-arc. So basically, my view is that the TV episodes are indeed "in the same canonicity" and that they are "what is happening in their minds during the Instrumentality sequence of End of Eva".....TO THE POINT that you could edit them into the Third Impact scene of EoE and it would fit perfectly. However, I'm also pointing out that: this was never meant to serve as a proper "ending"...for even if we were seeing "What was happening in Shinjij's mind" and not the real world, the TV ending would then extend 10 minutes longer to show Shinji's final conversation with Rei/Yui in EoE (which also happened "in his mind") at the end of Instrumentality. Even in the movie, for a time he accepts Instrumentality..........but THEN he rejects it, after talking with Rei one last time and realizing "it hurt when I was there but I know it was Real when it happened, so I want to go back" the TV ending is as if we just left you hanging at "he accepts Instrumentality...." recognizing that I just wrote a set of ellipses, which are finished by the movie's "....but despite the pain, I know my life was Real when it happened at that had value, so on SECOND THOUGHT, I reject this" does this mean the TV episodes are worthless? No. No more than the mind-trip scenes from episode 16 and Leliel or in episode 20. However, they are literally as if you took extra Instrumentality scenes from End of Eva and expanded on them. They do offer invaluable insight into the characters, and you should really consider them "a vital must-watch supplement to End of Eva"

[]:             Quote Post by AyrYntake » Wed Oct 01, 2008 2:00 pm There are those who consider EoE a second slap in the face after having watched EoTV...but I'm not one of them. I think that while EoE describes well what happened in the real world, you definitely need EoTV to give you some insight into what goes in within Shinji's mind. It gives you a better view of Instrumentality (after all, there wouldn't be some independent cameraman out there in space filming the ongoing events of EoE, so the viewer should be involved...get it?), and it also helps ease the mindfuck of EoE which would otherwise be whole half hours of incomprehensible, epileptic flashing. Notice also many of the hand-drawn motifs in EoTV reappearing in EoE (the egg, the cross, the eye etc.) in significant moments, where they represent pretty much the same thing. I believe that the events in EoTV were basically the same as those in the latter part of EoE, just from the viewpoint of an involved audience. All in all, I think EoTV as a supplement, as you said, is pretty good an idea. What I mildly disagree with is your suggestion that EoTV doesn't work as a stand-alone ending. It does , but you just need the right appreciative mindset. A book I once read described it as "getting to the end of a detective novel and being told that what's important isn't the murderer's identity, but the psychoanalysis and personality of the story's characters." Well, perhaps that's where Anno is trying to lead us. What's important in Eva ISN'T where the Angels came from, or why the heck we never got to see the MPEs, or anything else in the physical world. It's the spiritual bit that he actually wants to emphasize.

[]: Re: My personal resolution of the TV and movie endings             Quote Post by Silent Lightning » Wed Oct 01, 2008 2:03 pm That's all very well, but you need to reconcile it with that: V wrote: There are many answers out there I've seen for "how to reconcile they endingss"; some saying that they are alternate endings, some that they are the same "ending" viewed differently. I've noticed Aaron's position that, if you look at them, they cannot possibly be the same ending; I enjoy the analogy of Shinji's acceptance of Instrumentality in the TV ending, to Winston Smith's acceptance of Big Brother at the end of 1984.

[]:             Quote Post by AyrYntake » Wed Oct 01, 2008 2:13 pm Apologies. What I meant was that the events in EoTV were basically the same as those in the latter part of EoE, up to the point when Shinji accepts Instrumentality.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Oct 01, 2008 2:21 pm V wrote: compare Cypher and Neo's views on "The Matrix"; Cypher wants to be back in the make-believe happy-world where all is well; Neo prefers the world where there's a lot of fear and pain, but it is *Real*

[]:             Quote Post by AyrYntake » Wed Oct 01, 2008 2:30 pm I'm sure an amalgam-identity-GNR thing would actually work; it just has to be done willingly rather than by force as in EoE

[]:             Quote Post by V » Wed Oct 01, 2008 2:47 pm AyrYntake wrote: All in all, I think EoTV as a supplement, as you said, is pretty good an idea. What I mildly disagree with is your suggestion that EoTV doesn't work as a stand-alone ending. It does , but you just need the right appreciative mindset.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Wed Oct 01, 2008 2:56 pm No double posting

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Oct 01, 2008 2:59 pm No using all that annoying bold face in posts, either. This is, what, the fiftieth time I've brought this up, V? I also feel like everything I've said about making your posts more concise and coherent has fallen upon very deaf ears. Not good. Start trying harder.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Oct 01, 2008 3:09 pm V wrote: I have no idea, based on this cryptic and glib comment, if you are even in support of or opposed to my original statement. Please clarify.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Wed Oct 01, 2008 3:52 pm Reichu wrote: No using all that annoying bold face in posts, either. This is, what, the fiftieth time I've brought this up, V? I also feel like everything I've said about making your posts more concise and coherent has fallen upon very deaf ears. Not good. Start trying harder.

[]:             Quote Post by AyrYntake » Wed Oct 01, 2008 4:09 pm V wrote: Instrumentality isn't "transcending the mundane"; it's giving up. It's saying "let's artificially drop all that makes us people, rather than try to overcome our interpersonal boundaries"

[]:             Quote Post by mrpanda » Wed Oct 01, 2008 4:10 pm They are both alternate and supplementary, for me EoTV is the prefered ending as it explains everything in the characters heads and for me what goes on in their heads is the most important aspect of Eva. EoE is brilliant no doubt about it but the flashyness and fast paced action just doesnt reflect the series, sure it may have been what anno intended but i feel the ending where he had nothing but his creativity and some quick thinking.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Oct 01, 2008 4:17 pm To provide some more visual rhythm in a long post, you can use lists a b and horizontal rules for punctuation, and targeted use of bold , underscore and slightly larger text to make headlines, with italic text for light emphasis. Then breaking down the argument into personal history the facts of production a reading of the product conclusion The two endings can be seen as different emphases upon the same thing up to the moment when the illusion of Instrumentality is shattered; only the "Congratulations" scene is where it's difficult to reconcile them. My interpretation, conditioned by the same background as makes my strong reaction to EoE, is to liken the final state of EoTV to that of the natives of the polein in various of Greg Egan's SF works (e.g. Diaspora ).

[]:             Quote Post by LeoXiao » Wed Oct 01, 2008 5:46 pm EoE was like an upgrade that made everything make much more sense and ended the series with the traditional battle sequence, which was awesome. The original ending worked, but EoE made it much, much better.

[]:             Quote Post by Zaque » Wed Oct 01, 2008 6:07 pm V wrote: But you see, I think that was looking at it wrong, and I have a more nuanced view than what Aaron said: these aren't "alternate endings" but they're not "the same ending" either

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Wed Oct 01, 2008 6:24 pm Geez... lots to discuss here! EOE vs. EOTV: My personal take is that the two are more-or-less concurrent, but not in the manner V suggests. Rather, I think that the individual scenes in 25 occur either before instrumentality begins or during pre-instrumentality. 26 is instrumentality proper, though many of the characters (excluding Rei) are actually facets of Shinji arguing amongst himself rather than the presence of the actual other people (and also to let the rest of the voice cast get paid). I beleive that most of 26 deals specifically with the process of Shinji putting himself back together by "imagining himself in his own heart". Episode 25: Shinji 1= suicide attempt #2 > hears that Asuka is awake and fighting Asuka 1= thoughts while alone in the lake Rei = looking at moon > floating in LCL (exposition to explain vaguely what the hell is happening) Misato = P3II expanded, including Kaji sex scene Asuka 2= P3II expanded, including thoughts while confronting Shinji Shinji 2= "...then they can all just die" Episode 26: start to "Really?" = (more exposition) up to phone call = initial at fields dropping and massive rant of negativity up to end of hand-drawn = process of putting himself back together RiAO = (out of movie order) where is my dream/reality? > alternate world up to "Congratulations!" = end of putting self back together final text = actually reembodies, exiting 01 and leaving Yui behind. Matrix Tangent: I actually think the third movie massively dropped the ball in falling back on the messiah motif... and DBZ action didn't help matters much. It would have been -far- more profound if it had instead been revealed that the resistance base and bleak reality were in fact just another layer of the matrix, designed to be hopeless specifically to accommodate pessimist rebel types who don't accept the first layer. Essentially this would be saying that joining a counterculture group is just as fake an existence as just going along with the mainstream... "I'm an individual just like all my other HotTopic friends!" Thus Neo's true goal becomes to escape from this second layer and any that follow in the pursuit of his ultimate personal truth. Eventually I would have him discover that he is in fact the first self-aware AI program, having been put through this simulated hell by his creators as a feedback process to test his ability to adapt and grow, with the specific 'enemies' he faces designed to give him empathy with humans who might react to his eventual release into the larger world with fear. Essentially the whole thing would be a more tech-savy and action-oriented telling of The Truman Show . Posting pet peeves: In all fairness Reichu, your explanation in the other thread can reasonably be (mis)read as actually suggesting he use bold instead: Reichu wrote: Simple reason is that ***V has a pathological reliance*** on ****drawing attention to "important points" in the text when this isn't necessary in the first place .

[]:             Quote Post by Ornette » Wed Oct 01, 2008 6:31 pm http://evageeks.org/forum/faq.php?mode=bbcode linked from: When you make a post

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Wed Oct 01, 2008 8:39 pm Whatever, might as well throw my thoughts on the whole concurrency issue yet again for the lulz: Episode 25: Mentally occurring in Movie 25' up until.... Episode 25 when Shinji: What is this? I've felt like I've felt this before (Shinji turning into LCL) which corresponds with Shinji tanging in Movie 26'. From this point on, I see series episode 25 and 26 occurring during Pre and actual Instrumentality. The exact details of what's where are on another thread called Concurrency Project if you're that interested.

[]:             Quote Post by V » Wed Oct 01, 2008 9:00 pm Concurrency Project? Well, ultimately the gist of what I'm saying is that its not a one-for-one correspondance: the TV ending was never meant to be an "ending" because they were already planning End of Eva to make up for it; nor, even in hindsight, does it stand up as an "ending" without EoE. Rather, it is indeed happening "concurrently".....but....and here's the key point: it does not extend all the way to the final scene of End of Eva. The TV ending is indeed what is happening "in their minds" during EoE, with the key distinction, that it ends when Shinji accepts Instrumentality (which happens during EoE when we start getting the truly trippy scenes) but the TV episodes end BEFORE Shinji's final epiphany that "you know what? The real world is painful but it was "real" when I was with my friends there, so I'm going to reject Instrumentality and go back" (thus leaving Shinji and Asuka on a ruined beach overlooking a tang-colored ocean of LCL as the Rei/Lilith/Adam Hybrid's corpse rots on the horizon...then fast-forwarding, Shinji and Asuka "get it on" extensively, in a mind-blowing amount of angsty teenaged smex to repopulate the planet. I mean dogs in heat. I'm talking enough smex to make Cleopatra blush in modesty, angsty tears rolling down their cheeks in release only to peak in a shuddering-- (in case you can't tell, I'm trying to tease Sailor Stardust based on her earlier comments)-- then you cut ahead billions of years into the future beyond Third Impact, to find their distant descendants have "Rebuilt" everything more or less the same, and a new, "Rebuild-Shinji" and "Rebuild-Asuka" stand poised to awkwardly handle their mutual attraction and neuroses once again. Such is life )

[]: My personal resolution of the TV and movie endings             Quote Post by V » Wed Oct 01, 2008 1:45 pm just felt like putting this into words, but my personal "resolution" of the alternate TV and movie endings runs thusly: first off, there is only one "Evangelion movie"; Death & Rebirth is an artifact of the series release history. :             Quote Post by mrpanda » Wed Oct 01, 2008 4:10 pm They are both alternate and supplementary, for me EoTV is the prefered ending as it explains everything in the characters heads and for me what goes on in their heads is the most important aspect of Eva. []:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Oct 01, 2008 4:17 pm To provide some more visual rhythm in a long post, you can use lists a b and horizontal rules for punctuation, and targeted use of bold , underscore and slightly larger text to make headlines, with italic text for light emphasis. Then breaking down the argument into personal history the facts of production a reading of the product conclusion The two endings can be seen as different emphases upon the same thing up to the moment when the illusion of Instrumentality is shattered; only the "Congratulations" scene is where it's difficult to reconcile them. My interpretation, conditioned by the same background as makes my strong reaction to EoE, is to liken the final state of EoTV to that of the natives of the polein in various of Greg Egan's SF works (e.g. Diaspora ).

Article: My personal resolution of the TV and movie endings
 6:24 pm Geez... lots to discuss here! EOE vs. EOTV: My personal take is that the two are more-or-less concurrent, but not in the manner V suggests. Rather, I think that the individual scenes in 25 occur either before instrumentality begins or during pre-instrumentality. 26 is instrumentality proper, though many of the characters (excluding Rei) are actually facets of Shinji arguing amongst himself rather than the presence of the actual other people (and also to let the rest of the voice cast get paid). I beleive that most of 26 deals specifically with the process of Shinji putting himself back together by "imagining himself in his own heart". Episode 25: Shinji 1= suicide attempt #2 > hears that Asuka is awake and fighting Asuka 1= thoughts while alone in the lake Rei = looking at moon > floating in LCL (exposition to explain vaguely what the hell is happening) Misato = P3II expanded, including Kaji sex scene Asuka 2= P3II expanded, including thoughts while confronting Shinji Shinji 2= "...then they can all just die" Episode 26: start to "Really?" = (more exposition) up to phone call = initial at fields dropping and massive rant of negativity up to end of hand-drawn = process of putting himself back together RiAO = (out of movie order) where is my dream/reality? > alternate world up to "Congratulations!" = end of putting self back together final text = actually reembodies, exiting 01 and leaving Yui behind. Matrix Tangent: I actually think the third movie massively dropped the ball in falling back on the messiah motif... and DBZ action didn't help matters much. It would have been -far- more profound if it had instead been revealed that the resistance base and bleak reality were in fact just another layer of the matrix, designed to be hopeless specifically to accommodate pessimist rebel types who don't accept the first layer. Essentially this would be saying that joining a counterculture group is just as fake an existence as just going along with the mainstream... "I'm an individual just like all my other HotTopic friends!" Thus Neo's true goal becomes to escape from this second layer and any that follow in the pursuit of his ultimate personal truth. Eventually I would have him discover that he is in fact the first self-aware AI program, having been put through this simulated hell by his creators as a feedback process

Article: My personal resolution of the TV and movie endings
 escape from this second layer and any that follow in the pursuit of his ultimate personal truth. Eventually I would have him discover that he is in fact the first self-aware AI program, having been put through this simulated hell by his creators as a feedback process to test his ability to adapt and grow, with the specific 'enemies' he faces designed to give him empathy with humans who might react to his eventual release into the larger world with fear. Essentially the whole thing would be a more tech-savy and action-oriented telling of The Truman Show . Posting pet peeves: In all fairness Reichu, your explanation in the other thread can reasonably be (mis)read as actually suggesting he use bold instead: Reichu wrote: Simple reason is that ***V has a pathological reliance*** on ****drawing attention to "important points" in the text when this isn't necessary in the first place .

[]:             Quote Post by V » Wed Oct 01, 2008 9:00 pm Concurrency Project? Well, ultimately the gist of what I'm saying is that its not a one-for-one correspondance: the TV ending was never meant to be an "ending" because they were already planning End of Eva to make up for it; nor, even in hindsight, does it stand up as an "ending" without EoE. Rather, it is indeed happening "concurrently".....but....and here's the key point: it does not extend all the way to the final scene of End of Eva. The TV ending is indeed what is happening "

Article: My personal resolution of the TV and movie endings
 without EoE. Rather, it is indeed happening "concurrently".....but....and here's the key point: it does not extend all the way to the final scene of End of Eva. The TV ending is indeed what is happening "in their minds" during EoE, with the key distinction, that it ends when Shinji accepts Instrumentality (which happens during EoE when we start getting the truly trippy scenes) but the TV episodes end BEFORE Shinji's final epiphany that "you know what? The real world is painful but it was "real" when I was with my friends there, so I'm going to reject Instrumentality and go back" (thus leaving Shinji and Asuka on a ruined beach overlooking a tang-colored ocean of LCL as the Rei/Lilith/Adam Hybrid's corpse rots on the horizon...then fast-forwarding, Shinji and Asuka "get it on" extensively, in a mind-blowing amount of angsty teenaged smex to repopulate the planet. I mean dogs in heat. I'm talking enough smex to make Cleopatra blush in modesty, angsty tears rolling down their cheeks in release only to peak in a shuddering-- (in case you can't tell, I'm trying to tease Sailor Stardust based on her earlier comments)-- then you cut ahead billions of years into the future beyond Third Impact, to find their distant descendants have "Rebuilt" everything more or less the same, and a new, "Rebuild-Shinji" and "Rebuild-Asuka" stand poised to awkwardly handle their mutual attraction and neuroses once again. Such is life )
